title: Under the rivers
text:
What & Why
At the time I was dating someone living in Liege where the River Meuse goes through so I made this visual to see how, we looking at the river from our separate houses, we were looking at the same river. Afterwards I also added the Rhine river to see all the different places I’ve been to that are in the banks of these two rivers.

title: River Maas
text:
Approach
Briefly describe your analytical approach. What choices did you make and why? Mention any interesting data wrangling, statistical methods, or design decisions.
This is where you show you think, not just code.

title: River Maas
text:
Key Takeaways
What did the visualization reveal? What’s the main insight someone should take away from looking at it? 1-3 bullet points max.

Finding one
Finding two

title: River Maas
text:
Process Notes
(Optional — include this section if you want to show your learning process)
Anything you found tricky, a technique you learned, or something you’d do differently next time.
# You can include small code snippets to highlight interesting techniques
library(ggplot2)

# Example: the key plot code
ggplot(data, aes(x = var1, y = var2, color = group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#e07a5f", "#3d405b", "#81b29a")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Clear, descriptive title",
    subtitle = "Subtitle adds context",
    caption = "Source: dataset name"
  )

title: About
text:
I’m a data scientist working in pharmaceutical manufacturing, where I focus on process analytics and data science. My day-to-day involves turning complex manufacturing data into clear, actionable insights — often with R, ggplot2, and Shiny.
I care about making data visualizations that are not just accurate but genuinely useful and interesting to look at. I’m a regular participant in TidyTuesday and enjoy the challenge of finding stories in unfamiliar datasets.


I’m currently expanding into:

D3.js for interactive, web-native data visualization
Bayesian statistics & causal inference via Richard McElreath’s Statistical Rethinking
Data strategy and governance through a course at Imperial College and Simplilearn




Primary: R (tidyverse, ggplot2, Shiny) · Git · Quarto
Growing: Python (pandas, numpy) · D3.js · JavaScript · SQL
Domain: GxP environments · process analytics · statistical process control · equivalence testing · mixed models




GitHub
LinkedIn
Email: d.mendsev@gmail.com

title: Interrail trip February
text:
What & Why
I love traveling through Europe by train and every time I do I share on my social networks the maps of the trips I make. Initially I shared relatively simple maps made by the Rail Planner app but they didn’t look accurate enough. That took me down a rabbit hole I’m still in.

title: Interrail trip February
text:
Approach
I get the railway data from OSM, then look up the station coordinates using the DB API and use dodgr to calculate the shortest path from station to station along the railways This I lay over a political map of Europe. Then I use elevatr to get the elevation of each of the different pixels which I use for coloring the map.

title: Age gaps in movie couples across the years
text:
What & Why
I was curious about the age gap evolution through time wishing that they would narrow the closer we are to today. The message as always ended up being more complicated. The maximum age gap has been decreasing since the early 2000s

title: Age gaps in movie couples across the years
text:
Approach
The basic plot is a relatively straight forward scatterplot. The magic happened when checking actors that appear multiple times in the dataset and see the age of all their onscreen partners at the time of each of their film. The using color for showing each of the actors and text to give an explanation to the line joining the dots.

title: Desert Rodents
text:
What & Why
This graph is a bit of a busy one but it’s trying to give a vibe for lengths and weights of rodents, in general and per species. You can see overall and typical ranges and also have an idea for how chubby those mice are.

title: Desert Rodents
text:
Approach
Rodents are ordered left to right on alphabetical order while you can see boxplots and outliers for both length and weight in different facets, one on top of the other to allow for easy comparison.

title: Tornadoes on the Fried chicken chef states
text:
What & Why
The dataset contained a historical overview of tornadoes in the US and an meme came to memory where you could see that the states of Minnesota, Iowa, Missouri, Kentucky, Arkansas, Tennessee and Louisiana formed the silhouette of a chef with a plate of food on their hands. Since the food corresponds to the state of Kentucky, it made sense to think of it as fried chicken. I was also curious to see if the incidence of tornadoes had increase with time and it does appear so.

title: Tornadoes on the Fried chicken chef states
text:
Approach
You can see the aforementioned chef and next to each of the states, a barchart of number of tornadoes. Moreover, for higher magnitude tornadoes, a line is drawned from the coordinates where it started using color for magnitude and length for the miles it traveled.
